name: Misogi
shortDescription: Misogi is a Japanese Shinto purification ritual involving cleansing the body, often with water, to remove impurities, pollution (kegare), and spiritual defilement.
description: Misogi is a fundamental purification ritual within Shinto, the indigenous religion of Japan. It involves the ritualistic cleansing of the body, typically with water, to remove impurities, pollution (kegare), and spiritual defilement. Kegare can arise from various sources, including contact with death, illness, or even negative emotions. Misogi aims to restore the individual's purity and harmony with the kami (spirits or deities) and the natural world. The ritual is not simply a physical act of washing; it is a deeply spiritual practice intended to cleanse both the body and the spirit. There are various forms of misogi, ranging from simple ablutions with water to more elaborate practices such as standing under waterfalls (takigyo) or bathing in the sea. The Izanagi myth provides a foundational example of misogi, as Izanagi performed a purification ritual after his journey to Yomi, the land of the dead, to cleanse himself of the pollution he encountered there. Misogi is often performed before important Shinto ceremonies or pilgrimages, signifying a state of readiness and reverence. The concept is closely tied to the Shinto emphasis on purity, both physical and spiritual, as a prerequisite for interacting with the sacred. Similar concepts of ritual purification can be found in other religions and cultures, reflecting a universal human desire to cleanse oneself of defilement and reconnect with the divine.
